title: Apply: Elections and Voting: An Overview
description: Apply your understanding of elections and voting procedures in this multiple-choice exercise.
topic_path: Elections and Voting Rights: From the Founding through the Civil Rights Movement
Apply: Elections and Voting: An Overview

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Madison’s “Note to His Speech on the Right of Suffrage” (1821).**

The right of suffrage is a fundamental Article in Republican Constitutions. The regulation of it is, at the same time, a task of peculiar delicacy. Allow the right exclusively [[☃ definition 2]] property, and the rights of persons may be oppressed. The [[☃ definition 1]] alone sufficiently proves it. Extend it equally to all, and the rights of property or the claims of justice may be overruled by a majority without property, or interested in measures of injustice. Of this abundant proof is afforded by other popular [governments]and is not without examples in our own. . . .

In a just & a free, Government, therefore, the rights both of property & of persons ought to be [[☃ definition 3]] guarded. Will the former be so in case of a universal & equal suffrage? Will the latter be so in case of a suffrage confined to the holders of property?

**Which statement best summarizes Madison’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a society in which people worked for and swore loyalty to wealthy landowners in exchange for land and protection
in a way that produces the intended results

choices.content: We cannot restrict the right to vote to those who own property because doing so will lead to the oppression of the majority who do not own any property.
We must expand suffrage to as many groups as possible; the right to vote should not be restricted, as it is crucial to a free and fair republican government.
To ensure that people who do not own property do not oppress minority groups, we should not give any people without property the right to vote.
To protect the rights of all, we must find a fair balance between giving only property owners the right to vote and giving all people the right to vote.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Madison’s “Note to His Speech on the Right of Suffrage” (1821).**

The right of suffrage is a fundamental Article in Republican Constitutions. The regulation of it is, at the same time, a task of peculiar delicacy. Allow the right exclusively [[☃ definition 1]] property, and the rights of persons may be oppressed. The [[☃ definition 2]] alone sufficiently proves it. Extend it equally to all, and the rights of property or the claims of justice may be overruled by a majority without property, or interested in measures of injustice. Of this abundant proof is afforded by other popular [governments]and is not without examples in our own. . . .

In a just & a free, Government, therefore, the rights both of property & of persons ought to be [[☃ definition 3]] guarded. Will the former be so in case of a universal & equal suffrage? Will the latter be so in case of a suffrage confined to the holders of property?

**This excerpt could best be used as an example of a response to which historical development?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a society in which people worked for and swore loyalty to wealthy landowners in exchange for land and protection
in a way that produces the intended results

choices.content: Calls for the abolition of slavery
Arguments that the government should be able to seize private property for public use
Demands for universal suffrage for White men
Requests to change the process for electing senators

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 62 (1788) by James Madison.**

It is equally unnecessary to [[☃ definition 1]] the appointment of senators by the State legislatures. Among the various modes which might have been devised for constituting this branch of government, that which has been proposed by the convention is probably the most [[☃ definition 2]] the public opinion. It is recommended by the double advantage of favoring a [[☃ definition 3]] appointment, and of giving to the State governments such an agency in the formation of the federal government as must secure the authority of [[☃ definition 4]], and may form a convenient link between the two systems.

**Which statement best describes the method of electing senators, as described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: high-quality; carefully chosen based on suitability
the first thing mentioned (i.e., the state governments)

choices.content: An appointment in which the president selects two senators
A direct election in which the people vote for and elect two senators
An indirect election in which the state legislature selects the state’s two U.S. senators
A nomination process in which political parties select two senators

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 62 (1788) by James Madison.**

It is equally unnecessary to [[☃ definition 1]] the appointment of senators by the State legislatures. Among the various modes which might have been devised for constituting this branch of government, that which has been proposed by the convention is probably the most [[☃ definition 2]] the public opinion. It is recommended by the double advantage of favoring a [[☃ definition 3]] appointment, and of giving to the State governments such an agency in the formation of the federal government as must secure the authority of [[☃ definition 4]], and may form a convenient link between the two systems.

**Which amendment changed the method for choosing senators that Madison describes in the excerpt?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: high-quality; carefully chosen based on suitability
the first thing mentioned (i.e., the state governments)

choices.content: The Fifteenth Amendment
The Seventeenth Amendment
The Nineteenth Amendment
The Twenty-Second Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 59 (1788) by Alexander Hamilton.**

Nothing can be more evident, than that an exclusive power of regulating elections for the national Government, in the the hands of the State Legislatures, would leave the existence of the Union entirely at their mercy. They could at any moment [[☃ definition 1]] it by neglecting to provide for the choice of persons to administer its affairs.

**Which statement best summarizes Hamilton’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: If state governments regulate elections, they can elect officials who oppose each other and make a less effective government that experiences deadlock.
If the federal government can manage how states run their elections, it can make sure that states do not deny the right to vote to all citizens.
If state governments have sole responsibility for running national elections, they can ignore their responsibilities and threaten the country’s stability.
If the federal government can interfere in how states regulate elections, it can influence the results and elect officials who do not serve their states well.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Federalist No. 59 (1788) by Alexander Hamilton.**

Nothing can be more evident, than that an exclusive power of regulating elections for the national Government, in the the hands of the State Legislatures, would leave the existence of the Union entirely at their mercy. They could at any moment [[☃ definition 1]] it by neglecting to provide for the choice of persons to administer its affairs.

**Which provision of the Constitution most directly responds to the concerns that Hamilton expresses in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The popular election of representatives in the House
The indirect election of representatives in the Senate
The creation of the Electoral College
The power of Congress to regulate some aspects of federal elections

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from John Adams’s 1776 letter to James Sullivan, a judge in the Massachusetts Superior Court of Judicature.**

The same reasoning which will [[☃ definition 1]] you to admit all men who have no property to vote, with those who have . . . will prove that you ought to admit women and children. . . . Depend upon it, Sir, it is dangerous to open so fruitful a source of controversy and [[☃ definition 2]] as would be opened by attempting to alter the qualifications of voters; there will be no end of it. New claims will arise; women will demand the vote; lads from twelve to twenty-one will think their rights not attended to; and every man who has not a [[☃ definition 3]], will demand an equal voice with any other, in all acts of state. It tends to [[☃ definition 4]] and destroy all distinctions, and [[☃ definition 5]] all ranks to one common level.

**Which statement best summarizes Adams’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a type of coin, equal to a quarter of an old penny

choices.content: The government should give all people, including children, the right to vote.
The right to vote is fundamental to republican citizenship and cannot be denied.
If the government limits the right to vote to men who own property, democracy will fail.
If all men are given the right to vote, other groups will demand the right to vote.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an essay published in the Philadelphia *Independent Gazetteer* in 1787. The author uses the [[☃ definition 1]] An American Citizen.**

In pursuing the consideration of the new federal constitution, it remains now to examine the
nature and powers of the house of representatives—*the immediate delegates of the people.*

Each member of this truly [[☃ definition 2]] assembly will be chosen by about six thousand electors, *by the poor as well as the rich.* . . . As we shall have no [[☃ definition 4]] to purchase votes, so we shall have no votes for sale. . . .When the increasing population of the country shall [[☃ definition 3]] the body too large at the rate of one member for every thirty thousand persons, they will be returned at the greater rate of one for every forty or fifty thousand, which will render the electors still more incorruptible. 

For this regulation is only designed to prevent a *smaller number* than thirty thousand from having a representative. Thus we see a provision follows, that no state shall have less than one member; for if a new and greater number should hereafter be fixt on, which shall exceed the whole of the inhabitants of any state, such state, without this wholesome provision, would lose its voice in the house of representatives—A circumstance which the constitution renders *impossible.*

**Which section of the Constitution outlines the election process described above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: related to or concerning the people in general
government departments or the leaders of those departments as a group

choices.content: Article I, Section 2
Article I, Section 3
The Seventeenth Amendment
The Twenty-second Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Theophilus Parsons’s speech at the Massachusetts ratifying convention in 1788.**

But a state legislature, under the influence of their senators, who would have their fullest confidence, or under the influence of ambitious or popular characters, or in times of popular commotion, and when [[☃ definition 1]] and party spirit run high, would introduce such regulations as would [[☃ definition 2]] the rights of the people [[☃ definition 3]] and of little value. They might make an unequal and partial division of the State into districts for the election of representatives, or they might even disqualify one third of the electors. Without these powers in Congress, the people can have no remedy; But the 4th section [of Article I of the Constitution] provides a remedy, a controlling power in a legislature . . . without the influence of our commotions and factions, who will hear [[☃ definition 4]], and preserve and restore to the people their equal and sacred rights of election.

**According to Parsons, what is the purpose of the Elections Clause in Article I, Section 4 of the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: without taking personal opinions or biases into account

choices.content: To preserve the fairness of national elections and protect against potential corruption
To ensure that all citizens have the right to vote in national elections, regardless of their wealth
To make sure that all people running for office are not corrupt or looking to rebel against the government
To give states the power to check the national government by giving them more power over when and where elections are held

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an essay written by Luther Martin, the Maryland Delegate at the Constitutional Convention. The essay was published in the Baltimore *Maryland Gazette* in 1788.**

The connection between the representative and the represented, ought to be as near and as close as possible; according to these principles, Mr. Speaker, in this State it is provided by its constitution, that the representatives in Congress, shall be chosen annually, shall be paid by the State, and shall be subject to recall even within the year; so cautiously has our constitution guarded against an abuse of the trust [[☃ definition 1]] our representatives in the federal government; whereas by the third and sixth sections of the first article of this new [[☃ definition 2]], the senators are to be chosen for six years instead of being chosen annually; instead of being paid by their States who send them, they [[☃ definition 3]] with the other branch, are to pay themselves out of the treasury of the United States; and are not [[☃ definition 4]] to be recalled during the period for which they are chosen—

Thus, Sir, for six years the senators are [[☃ definition 5]] totally and absolutely independent of their States, of whom they ought to be the representatives, without any bond or tie between them—During that time they may join in measures ruinous and destructive to their States, even such as should totally [[☃ definition 6]] their State governments, and their States cannot recall them, nor exercise any control over them.

**Which statement best summarizes Martin’s argument in the excerpt?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: that, the system described in the proposed Constitution

choices.content: The different rules for the Senate and the House of Representatives will mean that the Senate can endlessly debate bills.
The proposed terms, pay, and removal process for senators will mean that they are not accountable to the people in their states.
The powers of the Senate, as outlined in Article I, Section 6, far exceed the powers of the states, making the federal government too powerful.
The state legislatures have the power to select U.S. senators, giving too much power to the states and none to the Senate’s constituents.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Nathan Sanford’s speech at the New York state constitutional convention in 1821.**

The question before us is the right of [[☃ definition 1]]—who shall, or who shall not, have the right to vote. The committee have presented the scheme they thought best; to [[☃ definition 2]] all existing distinctions [dw: differences between people or how they’re treated] and make the right of voting uniform. Is this not right? Where did these distinctions arise? They arose from British [[☃ definition 3]]. . . . The principle of the scheme now proposed is that those who bear the burdens of the state should choose those that rule it. There is no privilege given to property, as such; but those who contribute to the public support, we consider as entitled to share in the election of rulers. . . .

**Which statement best summarizes the change that Sanford argues for in the speech?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: customs or ideas from the past that influence what people do now

choices.content: The increased use of British precedents in American election laws
The elimination of property ownership as a voting qualification
The inclusion of Black men as citizens of the United States
The use of literacy tests to determine who could vote

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt of a letter published in the *Maryland Gazette* in 1776. The author uses the [[☃ definition 1]] Watchman.**

Is it not an insult to common sense to say that a government can be formed by the authority of the people only, when near half of them are excluded from any share in the election of the convention which is to form the government? This inequality of representation, contained in the [[☃ definition 2]], cannot be justified on any principle. . . . The ultimate [[☃ definition 3]] of all freedom is the enjoyment of a free [[☃ definition 4]]. A constitution formed without this important right of free voting being preserved to the people, would be [[☃ definition 5]]. . . . 

Every poor man has a life, a personal liberty, and a right to his earnings, and is in danger of being injured by government in a variety of ways; therefore it is necessary that these people should enjoy the right of voting for representatives, to be protectors of their lives, and personal liberty, and their little property, which, though small, is yet, upon the whole, a very great object to them. 

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Denying people the right to vote based on property ownership is undemocratic.
Limiting the right to vote to men with property is the only way to protect democracy.
There are not enough representatives in Congress to properly address poor people’s needs.
The Constitutional Convention should appoint delegates to represent poor people.
